coredata.dc:title: Manganese-doped carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive sensing of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol via an inner filtering effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Huanhuan
Xiang, Guo Qiang
Wang, Yule
Zhang, Heng
Ning, Keke
Duan, Junyue
He, Lijun
Jiang, Xiuming
Zhao, Wenjie
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, a selective and sensitive method for detecting TNP using manganese doped carbon quantum dots (Mn-CDs) was developed. The Mn-CDs were prepared via a simple hydrothermal method using 1-(2-pyridinylazo)-2-naohthalenol naohthalenol (PAN) and MnCl2 as precursors. The as-prepared Mn-CDs have UV emission with high quantum yield (83.2%). Because of the strong characteristic absorption of TNP at 356 nm, which has good spectral overlap with the emission peak of Mn-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of Mn-CDs at 360 nm is linearly quenched in the presence of TNP in the concentration range of 0.1–200 μM. The developing assay based on an inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism for detecting TNP is selective, convenient, and shows that the as-prepared Mn-CDs have application prospects for simple and specific analytical chemistry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Trinitrophenol
Inner filtering effect
Water samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection Procedure for Fluorescent Sensing of TNP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Typical TEM of the prepared Mn-CDs. (b) FT-IR spectra of the prepared Mn-CDs and PCDs. (c) C1s and (d) Mn2p high resolution XPS spectra of Mn-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of prepared Mn-CDs and PCDs. Emission spectra of Mn-CDs at excitation wavelengths from (b) 250–350 nm and (c) 350–410 nm. (d) Effects of pH on fluorescence intensity of the Mn-CDs.

Fluorescence quenching of Mn-CDs by TNP. (a) Fluorescence responses of the Mn-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of TNP (0, 0.1, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100, 150, and 200 μM). (b) Dependence of I0/I on the concentration of TNP. Inset: linear relationship between lg(I0/I) and concentration of TNP.

Detection mechanism of TNP. (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of all the nitroaromatics and the fluorescence emission spectra of Mn-CDs. (b) Fluorescence decay profiles of Mn-CDs in the absence and presence of TNP.
Interferences of (a) other nitroaromatics and (b) metal ions in BR buffer (pH 3.0). Concentrations of TNP, nitroaromatics, and metal ions are 100 μM (10 μM for substances indicated with *).
Comparative data from some recent studies on fluorescent probes for TNP.

row.entry: Electron transfer and inner filter effect
Hydrogen-bonding interactions and resonance energy transfer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of TNP in real samples (n = 3, mean ± SD, μM).

head.ce:title: Manganese-doped carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive sensing of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol via an inner filtering effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of technology, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, a selective and sensitive method for detecting TNP using manganese doped carbon quantum dots (Mn-CDs) was developed. The Mn-CDs were prepared via a simple hydrothermal method using 1-(2-pyridinylazo)-2-naohthalenol naohthalenol (PAN) and MnCl2 as precursors. The as-prepared Mn-CDs have UV emission with high quantum yield (83.2%). Because of the strong characteristic absorption of TNP at 356 nm, which has good spectral overlap with the emission peak of Mn-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of Mn-CDs at 360 nm is linearly quenched in the presence of TNP in the concentration range of 0.1–200 μM. The developing assay based on an inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism for detecting TNP is selective, convenient, and shows that the as-prepared Mn-CDs have application prospects for simple and specific analytical chemistry.

ce:section.ce:para: 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (TNP, also named picric acid) is recognized as a primary ingredient of explosive materials, and it has a higher explosive power than 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) [1,2]. TNP has also been widely used in a variety of fields, including explosives manufacturing, leather, fireworks, pharmaceuticals, and dye industries [3–6]. However, TNP causes skin and eye irritation, headaches, aplastic anemia, liver damage, and male infertility [7,8]. Because of its high water solubility, TNP can easily lead to contamination of soil and groundwater, and thus it poses a serious health hazard [9]. Furthermore, because of its highly electron-deficient nature, TNP does not easily biodegrade. The permissible level for TNP in drinking water is set at 0.5 mg L−1 with an allowable daily intake of 1–37 μg (kg per day)−1 [10]. Therefore, sensitive and selective detection of TNP has attracted increasing attention [11–14].

Selective and sensitive detection of TNP in the presence of structurally similar interference compounds, such as dinitrotoluene (DNT), nitrobenzene (NB), and especially TNT, is an important and challenging task. These interference compounds and TNP have extremely similar chemical structures and properties [15]. To date, a number of analytical methods, such as surface enhanced Raman spectrometry [16], high performance liquid chromatography [17], mass spectrometry [18], dynamic light scattering [19], enzyme linked immunosorbent assay [20], and electrochemical methods, have been used for detecting nitroaromatic explosives [21]. However, these methods suffer from some drawbacks, such as high cost, complex operation of sophisticated instruments, complicated sample pretreatment procedures, and especially low distinguishability between TNP and TNT, and these drawbacks greatly limit their practical applications. In contrast, fluorescent-based methods have drawn much attention because of their high sensitivity, good selectivity, short response time, operational simplicity, and low cost [22,23]. Therefore, a series of fluorescence methods based on nanocomposites, such as silicon nanoparticles [24], polyimide covalent organic nanosheets [25], guanidine functionalized upconversion fluorescent nanoparticles [26], cadmium-pamoate metal-organic framework [27], copper nanoclusters [28] and carbon dots (CDs) [29–31], have been developed for the detecting TNP.

CDs are a new class of brightly photoluminescent nanomaterials and have gained wide attention because of their superior chemical stability, biocompatibility, toxicity, and tunable photoluminescence compared to luminescent quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes [32,33]. These excellent properties have led to successful applications of CDs in biological imaging and detection [34–37].

Herein, we demonstrate the synthesis and performance of a highly sensitive TNP nanosensing material based on Mn-doped carbon dots (Mn-CDs) synthesized using 1-(2-pyridinylazo)-2-naohthalenol (PAN) and MnCl2 as precursors. The as-prepared Mn-CDs have an emission band (360 nm) in the ultraviolet region; this emission band has a good spectral overlap with the absorption band of TNP at 356 nm, which makes TNP have a strong inner filtering effect (IFE) on the fluorescence of Mn-CDs. The fluorescence intensity of Mn-CDs at 360 nm is linearly quenched with an increased concentration of TNP. Performance of the analytical method based on this IFE was carefully evaluated, and the method was successfully applied with satisfactory results to the detection of TNP in water samples.

A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, F-6000) was used to record emission spectra. The fluorescence lifetime of the CDs was measured with a FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France). A JEM-2011 TEM was used to capture transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. Elemental composition of the CDs was measured with a Thermo escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in KBr were collected on a WQF-510 FT-IR spectrometer (Beijing Rayleigh Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2450).

PAN, TNP, TNT, nitrobenzene (NB), phenol (PHE), methylbenzene (MB), p-nitrotoluene (p-NT), hydroquinone (HQ), p-chlorophenol (p-CP), trichlorophenol (TCP), and p-aminophenol (p-AP) were purchased from Aladdin (www.aladdin-e.com, China). Manganese chloride tetrahydrate and other metal salts were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solutions were used to control the acidity of testing solutions. All of the chemical regents were analytically pure and used without further purification. TNT, TNP, and other explosives used in the present study are highly explosive and were handled only in small quantities. Ultrapure water (18 MΩ cm−1) was used in all of the experiments.

Mn-CDs were fabricated according to a previously reported procedure with a slight modification [38]. PAN (49.85 mg) and manganese chloride (0.4 mmol) were added to 10 mL of ethanol, and the mixture was stirred for 10 min. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon autoclave; the autoclave was tightly sealed and heated to 180 °C, and this temperature was maintained for 4 h. After reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The product was mixed with 20 mL of ultrapure water, and then the insoluble substance was removed via centrifugation at 11000 rpm for 30 min. After centrifugation, the resulting product was dried via lyophilization to obtain Mn-CDs powder. PCDs were fabricated using the same synthetic protocol as the Mn-CDs except without the addition of manganese chloride salts. The obtained Mn-CDs and PCDs powders were dispersed in water (0.25 mg mL−1) for further characterization and analytical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection Procedure for Fluorescent Sensing of TNP
ce:section.ce:para: First, a 1.0 mL solution of Mn-CDs (0.25 mg mL−1) was placed in a 10 mL graduated tube containing 5.0 mL of B-R buffer solution (pH 3.0). A certain amount of the standard solution of TNP (or the sample solution) was added to the test tube. The solution was diluted with ultrapure water to the required volume, and then the mixture was thoroughly shaken; the fluorescence of the test solutions was then recorded. Also, a blank sample was prepared without addition of the TNP standard solution or sample solution.

Fluorescence intensities of the emission peaks (360 nm) for the test solution (I) and blank (I0) were directly measured using a Shimadzu F-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 300 nm. Spectrometric quantities were calculated according to lg(I0/I).

A TEM image of the morphology of the obtained Mn-CDs is shown in Fig. 1a (TEM image of the PCDs was provided in Fig. S1). All of the samples have a typical spherical shape with a narrow size distribution, and it can be seen that the Mn-CDs have a uniform dispersion without any apparent aggregation. FT-IR spectra were used to identify the functional groups present on the Mn-CDs and PCDs. As shown in Fig. 1b, peaks attributed to typical functional groups (such as CN, pyridyl CH, and CC) in the Mn-CDs became weaker, and the wavenumbers of some peaks also changed slightly. This may be attributed to coordination band between Mn and pyridyl N.

XPS was used to find the elemental state and chemical composition of Mn-CDs. The high resolution C1s (Fig. 1c) spectrum can be resolved into four peaks and indicates the presence of 284.6 eV (CC), 285.5 eV (CC), 286.8 eV (CO/CO), and 287.7 eV (CN) [39]. The high resolution Mn2p (Fig. 1d) spectrum reveals the presence of Mn(II)-coordination in the Mn-CDs. The peak at 641.0 eV indicates the presence of MnO, and this proves the coordination of Mn⋯O is present.

Optical characteristics of Mn-CDs were explored using UV–visible spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 2a shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of the PCDs and Mn-CDs. Each spectrum shows a typical peak, at 289 nm for the PCDs and at 300 nm for the Mn-CDs. The absorption feature at 289 nm was assigned to the n-π* transition of the CO group. This absorption peak has a red shift, which is attributed to coordination of Mn⋯O.

Under excitation wavelengths in the range of 250–410 nm, the as-prepared Mn-CDs show excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence emissions, and the maximum emission is at 360 nm with a maximum excitation at 300 nm. Fig. 2b shows the fluorescence emission spectra when the Mn-CDs were irradiated at different excitation wavelengths (250–330 nm). Intensity of fluorescence emission increased with an increase in the excitation wavelength in the range of 250–300 nm; also, as the excitation wavelength was further increased from 310 nm to 410 nm, the FL emission wavelength was red shifted from 360 nm to 480 nm (Fig. 2c). From the emission spectra, it can be concluded that the maximum FL emission intensity was obtained at 360 nm with an excitation wavelength of 300 nm in aqueous solution. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the PCDs and Mn-CDs were measured according to a typical procedure, specifically, the slope method. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54 excited at 360 nm) was chosen as a reference. The QY of the Mn-CDs (83.2%) was much larger than that of the PCDs (2.4%). An improvement in QY after doping with heteroatoms was consistent with the literature [37]. And PCDs has the similar fluorescence emission behavior with the Mn-CDs (Fig. S2).

Further, effects of pH on fluorescence intensity of the Mn-CDs were investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 2d. Fluorescence intensities in acidic media were much higher than those in basic media; Fluorescence intensity remained almost constant in the acidic pH range of 2–4 and in the basic pH range of 7–11.

The as-prepared Mn-CDs show great importance in highly selective and sensitive detection of TNP. As shown in Fig. 3 , fluorescence intensity at 360 nm of the Mn-CDs can be effectively quenched in the presence of TNP. A linear relationship is achieved in the range of 1–200 μM. The detection limit is calculated to be 0.019 μM based on 3σ. In addition, fluorescence quenching was analyzed using the Sterm-Volmer equation[b]: I0/I = Ksv[TNP] + 1, where I0 and I are fluorescence intensities without and with added TNP, [TNP] is the molar concentration of TNP, and Ksv is the quenching constant (M−1). The obtained value of Ksv was 3.70 × 104 L mol−1. As shown in Fig. 3b, the linear equation between lg(I0/I) and concentration of TNP (C, μM) was lg(I0/I) = 0.00912C + 0.00664. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.6% (c = 50 μM).

The mechanism for efficient fluorescence quenching of Mn-CDs by TNP was strong IFE between Mn-CDs and TNP. TNP has strong characteristic absorption at 356 nm (ε = 1.05 × 104 cm−1mol−1L) and shows precise overlap with the emission spectrum of the Mn-CDs (Fig. 4a). Therefore, the observed fluorescence quenching of CDs by TNP may have resulted from IFE. Before exploring the question of whether the fluorescence quenching of Mn-CDs was caused by IFE, the fluorescence spectra should be corrected first. Recently, a simplified correction factor based on mathematical model of IFE has been deduced and widely used to calculate the deviation of IFE [39–41]. The correction can be expressed by the following equation:
I corr
/
I obse
=
10
A ex
x +
A em
y

Here, x and y represent the optical path length of excitation and emission beams while Aex and Aem denote the absorbance per centimeter at the excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively. From the absorbance at the excitation wavelength and emission wavelength, the corrected fluorescence intensities of Mn-CDs were obtained. After accurate calculation and analysis, it was found that the corrected fluorescence intensities of Mn-CDs almost remained unchanged with increasing TNP concentrations. Therefore, it can be concluded that the fluorescence decrease of CDs by TNP upon mixing was mainly attributed to IFE rather than static quenching. To further exclude the presence of static quenching, the absorption spectra of Mn-CDs, TNP and Mn-CDs-TNP mixtures were examined. When TNP was added, no significant difference was observed between the absorption spectra of Mn-CDs-TNP mixtures and those of the sum value of Mn-CDs and TNP (Fig. S3). The results indicated that no Mn-CDs-TNP complex was formed and the fluorescence decrease of Mn-CDs mainly resulted from IFE.

Selectivity of the fluorescent sensor for TNP was evaluated via determination of TNP in the presence of interfering substances. As shown in Fig. 5a, 100 μM TNP and other nitroaromatic compounds (TNT, NB, PHE, MB, p-NT, HQ, p-CP, TCP and p-AP) were selected to investigate the fluorescence performance of the Mn-CDs. Compared with the control group, fluorescence quenching behavior of Mn-CDs toward TNP in the presence of competitive compounds (100 μM; 10 μM for PNT) remains similar, and this indicates the high specificity of the sensor for detecting TNP.

Metal ions were also used to investigate the selectivity of TNP detection (Fig. 5b). None of the metal ions (100 μM; 10 μM for Fe3+) had an influence on fluorescence intensity of Mn-CDs in the presence and absence of TNP. Therefore, high tolerance concentrations for metal ions could be the basis for detecting TNP in water samples.

For comparative purpose, the performance characteristics of this method and other fluorescent probes for TNP reported in the literature were listed in Table 1 . It could be seen that many kinds of fluorescent probes were designed for TNP with different sensing mechanisms. And this method based on the Mn doped CDs through IFE mechanism has relative lower detection limit with wider linear range. Another thing should also be pointed out that CDs are often synthesized by simple one pot hydrothermal method and analytical procedure based on IFE has very short analytical time. Therefore, this method was a sensitive, selective analytical procedure for TNP with much convenience and short analytical time.

River water and snow were collected in Zhengzhou city (Henan province, China). Dye wastewater was obtained from Environmental Laboratory in Henan University of Technology (Zhengzhou, China). Water samples were analyzed without further treatments.

The proposed method was used to detect TNP in water samples by detecting recoveries after given amounts of TNP. Analytical results are shown in Table 2 , and the obtained recoveries are from 94.4% to 108.9%. Based on the good stability and anti-interference ability of the Mn-CDs, recovery results reveal the high reproducibility and accuracy of this approach.

Mn-CDs were prepared via hydrothermal reaction using PAN and MnCl2 as precursors. The Mn-CDs were used as an efficient turn-off fluorescent chemosensor for selective and sensitive TNP determination via IFE. The developed method had an analytical linear range from 0.1–200 μM with a detection limit of 0.012 μM. The current work demonstrates a direct and simple method for fabricating CDs and using CDs as a fluorescent probe for TNP sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21205028), the Program for Science and Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (Grant No. 13HASTIT016), and the Foundation for Young Core Teachers of Henan University of Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.07.006.
